nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or standing water: swamps, flooded forests, dense vegetation margins of rivers and lakes; often found in shallow, oxygen-poor waters where it breathes air via its gas bladder

decorPreferences: caves and PVC tubes for daytime hiding
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
dense marginal planting (Java fern, Anubias, Vallisneria)
dark substrate (fine sand or fine gravel)
tightly fitting lid — moderate escape risk

diet.notes: Primarily piscivorous in the wild; in the aquarium accepts meaty foods including frozen bloodworms, earthworms, river shrimp, chopped mussel, and prawns. Most specimens can be trained onto high-protein pellets or carnivore sticks with patience. Avoid feeder fish to reduce disease risk; use gut-loaded invertebrates instead. Feed after lights-out to suit nocturnal habits. Will consume tankmates small enough to swallow.

goodWith: large robust tetras (Congo tetra, larger Distichodus)
rope fish / ropefish (Erpetoichthys calabaricus) where size is compatible
avoidWith: small fish (will be eaten — any fish under ~8 cm)
boisterous or nippy species that may harass hiding knifefish

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Xenomystus nigri; standard reference photo. Confirm license before use.

summary: The African Knifefish is the sole species in genus Xenomystus — a slender, scaleless nocturnal predator from West and Central African swamps reaching ~30 cm. It ‘barks’ via gas-bladder vibration, is copper-sensitive, and requires hiding spots and meaty foods in a 250 L+ tank.
careNotes:
The African Knifefish (Xenomystus nigri) is the only member of its genus and one of the smaller knifefish available in the hobby, typically reaching 25–30 cm — modest compared to the Clown Knifefish (Chitala ornata, up to 100 cm) but still requiring significant space. A minimum of 250 L with a footprint of at least 120 cm is recommended to provide adequate territory and swimming room alongside caves and hiding structures. Being strictly nocturnal, this species will spend daylight hours concealed in caves, pipes, or dense vegetation; without adequate retreats it becomes chronically stressed and prone to disease. Lighting should be kept dim or supplemented with floating plants to diffuse surface light.

As a scaleless fish, X. nigri is highly sensitive to copper-based medications and salt treatments; any medication used in a tank housing this species must be verified copper-free. The species is an obligate air-breather — it surfaces regularly to gulp air via its modified gas bladder, which doubles as a primitive accessory respiratory organ. Unusually, it also uses this gas bladder to produce audible ‘barking’ or grunting sounds, particularly during territorial interactions or spawning activity, earning it the informal nickname ‘barking knife.’

Feeding should occur after lights-out. A varied diet of frozen bloodworms, earthworms, river shrimp, and chopped meaty foods is ideal; many specimens can be trained onto sinking carnivore pellets over time. Feeder fish should be avoided to reduce parasite and disease introduction. Territorial aggression toward conspecifics and other knifefish is intense — keep only one per tank unless the aquarium is extremely large with visual barriers. Any tankmate small enough to fit in its mouth will eventually be eaten. Water should be soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), and well-filtered with gentle flow to replicate its swamp habitat. Regular partial water changes of 25–30% weekly are important to maintain water quality.

Note: This species is sometimes mislabelled or confused with the Aba Aba Knifefish (Gymnarchus niloticus), which is a completely different family (Gymnarchidae), is weakly electric, and grows to over 160 cm — making it wholly unsuitable for home aquaria.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. Spawning has been reported in very large, heavily planted setups where a pair deposits adhesive eggs on broad-leaved plants or flat surfaces. The male guards the eggs. Precise breeding triggers are not well documented in the aquarium literature; seasonal temperature and rainfall simulation (lowering temperature slightly then raising, combined with heavy feeding and water changes) may stimulate spawning. Larvae are difficult to raise and require very small live foods. Breeding is considered uncommon to rare in the hobby.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Xenomystus nigri
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~20 cm standard length / up to 30 cm TL), temperature 23–28°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 1–12 dGH, native range (West and Central Africa), habitat (swamps, slow-moving waters), nocturnal behaviour, diet (carnivore/piscivore tendency), air-breathing behaviour, copper sensitivity, territorial aggression, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Xenomystus nigri
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Notopteridae, Osteoglossiformes), adult size (to 30 cm TL), native range countries, diet (carnivore), habitat (swamps and slow-flowing waters), and gas-bladder respiratory/sound-production function. Confirmed sole member of genus Xenomystus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — African Knifefish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-focused care guide. Confirmed tank size recommendation (55+ gal / ~208 L minimum, 75+ gal / ~280 L preferred), nocturnal feeding advice, cave and hiding requirements, diet (frozen bloodworms, earthworms, carnivore pellets), copper sensitivity, and scaleless body caution for medications. Size reported as up to 12 inches (30 cm).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Knifefish species guide
additional.notes: Contextualised X. nigri among knifefish group; confirmed smaller size relative to Clown Knifefish, nocturnal piscivore habits, need for caves, aggression toward conspecifics, and note on confusion with Gymnarchus niloticus (Aba Aba). Supported ‘intermediate’ care level assessment.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Notopterid Knifefishes
additional.notes: Supplementary source on Notopteridae family husbandry; gas-bladder sound production (‘barking knife’ behaviour), air-breathing adaptation, territorial behaviour, and West/Central African distribution noted. Supported breeding rarity assessment.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. Egg-scatterer on broad leaves or flat surfaces with paternal egg-guarding reported. Precise conditioning and spawning triggers not well established; fry require micro live foods. Breeding events in home aquaria are uncommon.
